📍 The Site
    ⏱ Your Task — 18 Minutes
    • Click any zone marker (Z1–Z11) on the map to open its card menu
    • Select a card to place it — a coloured dot appears on the map
    • Stay within the J$18,500,000 budget — the bar in the header tracks your spend
    • When done, hit Summary to see your pitch view
    🎯 Your 3-Min Pitch Covers
    • Which NbCS cards you chose and where you placed them
    • The primary hazard your package addresses
    • The strongest co-benefit in your design
    • One constraint you had to navigate — and how
    🔵 Card Colour Key
    • Blue — Water & Flood (cards 1, 2, 3, 8, 9)
    • Green — Heat & Greening (cards 4, 6)
    • Pink — Flood + Heat dual (cards 5, 7)
    • Orange — Food & Livelihoods (cards 10, 11)
    • Yellow — Sustainability (card 12)

    Pitch Guide — 3 Minutes

    1Which NbCS you chose and where you placed them

    🌀 PHASE 2 UNLOCK
    Enter the hurricane code from your facilitator to reveal the shock event.
    Incorrect code. Check with your facilitator.
    ⚠ EMERGENCY ALERT — NATIONAL WEATHER SERVICE
    🌀
    BREAKING NEWS

    Hurricane Iris
    Category 5

    Tropical Storm Iris has rapidly intensified. The storm is projected to make landfall in approximately 96 hours and is expected to directly impact your urban corridor.

    🌧 Rainfall exceeding records
    🌊 Significant flash flooding
    💨 Winds exceeding 250 km/h
    ⚡ Infrastructure disruption
    💰 Emergency diversion of funds
    Government agencies have declared a national emergency.
    YOUR HURRICANE IMPACT CHALLENGES
    IMPACT 1 — BUDGET REDUCTION
    Your project budget is reduced by 20%
    The Ministry of Finance has redirected emergency resources. Teams must immediately revise their plans.
    Revised budget: —
    IMPACT 2 — REGULATORY FAST-TRACK
    Emergency legislation — fast-track ONE intervention only
    Justify: (1) Why this intervention should be prioritised, and (2) What resilience benefits it delivers.
    IMPACT 3 — SUPPLY CHAIN DISRUPTION
    Your assigned disruption card
    Loading…
    IMPACT 4 — COMMUNITY PRESSURE
    Residents demand immediate visible protection
    Decide: Continue long-term NbS? Shift to short-term protection? Or balance both?
    TEAM DECISION QUESTIONS
    Which projects do you keep, modify, postpone, or cancel?
    Which regulatory hurdle becomes your greatest challenge?
    How do you justify spending limited resources during an emergency?
    Which vulnerable populations are most affected by your decisions?
    How will your NbS investments perform during and after the hurricane?
    If you had known the hurricane was coming, what would you have designed differently?
    PHASE 3 — POST-HURRICANE ASSESSMENT
    NbCS Performance Scorecard
    THE HURRICANE HAS PASSED. YOUR CORRIDOR EXPERIENCED:
    ⛈ Severe flooding 🌳 Significant tree loss 🏗 Damage to some NbS infrastructure
    However, some interventions performed better than expected.
    Bioswale / Lined Drain
    🟢 High
    Designed to capture, slow, and infiltrate stormwater runoff. Significantly reduces peak runoff and flooding if properly sized and maintained. Vegetation stabilises soils and reduces erosion. Recovery rate is quick.
    📎 Case Study: St. George Rainway, Vancouver →
    Permeable Paving
    🟢 High
    Allows stormwater infiltration and reduces surface runoff. Remains effective during heavy rainfall if regularly maintained and not clogged by storm-generated debris. (e.g. permeable asphalt, grass-crete)
    📎 Case Study: Green City, Clean Waters — BC/Ontario →
    Green Roof
    🟢 Moderate–High
    Reduces runoff by retaining rainfall, lowers building temperatures, continues functioning during storms when properly engineered. Performance remains strong unless wind damage exceeds design specifications.
    📎 Case Study: Green Roof Stormwater Management, Seoul →
    Rain Gardens / Retention Basin
    🟡 Moderate
    Effective for moderate rainfall but may become overwhelmed during prolonged or extreme hurricane rainfall. Performance depends on soil infiltration rates and storage capacity — limited volume during prolonged events.
    📎 Case Study: Toronto Green Streets Program →
    Community Garden / Pocket Park
    🟡 Moderate
    Provide localised flood storage and cooling benefits but limited in scale. May become temporarily flooded or damaged — water sits on the surface before percolating, reducing immediate effectiveness.
    📎 Case Study: NbS in Small Island Developing States, Caribbean →
    Street Tree Planting
    🟠 Mixed
    Mature trees provide cooling, stormwater interception, and erosion control. However, hurricane-force winds can uproot or damage trees — particularly where species selection, maintenance, or rooting conditions are inadequate.
    📎 Case Study: Toronto Urban Forest Strategy →
    A corridor with well-chosen, properly sized, and maintained NbS interventions should show limited damage and faster recovery — mirroring real-world challenges faced by municipalities, planners, donors, and developers implementing nature-based solutions in climate-vulnerable urban areas.
